FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study wave 1 release with 6758 individuals in main text and abstract.

FT_IC2.evidence: Reports 'more screen time' (F=6.80, p<.0001) as a characteristic differentiating profiles but does not measure a specific smartphone or social media variable. Screen time is mentioned descriptively without phone/SM breakdown or separate variable extraction.

FT_IC3.evidence: No inferential statistical test linking a specific phone/SM variable to a non-SM outcome. Screen time is only reported as a descriptive characteristic across profile groups.

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric coefficient (β/OR/r/p) reported for a smartphone/SM association.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using latent profile analysis on ABCD fMRI data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Journal article published in Biological Psychiatry: Cognitive Neuroscience and Neuroimaging, 2022.

FT_EC7_reason: FT_IC2 and FT_IC3 not met - no specific phone/SM variable measured or analyzed.
notes: Paper focuses on neurodevelopmental profiles via fMRI. Screen time is mentioned as a correlate of profile membership but is neither the main exposure nor analyzed as a specific variable. Does not meet inclusion criteria (fails FT-IC2 and FT-IC3).

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study COVID-19 survey (May 2020 COVID-19 Rapid Response Research Release) with 5412 adolescents aged 10-14.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific screen modalities: texting, social media, video chatting, multiplayer gaming, single-player gaming, and streaming videos/movies/television. Adolescents self-reported hours on typical day excluding schoolwork.

FT_IC3.evidence: Multiple linear regression analyses with mental health and resiliency factors (eMethods) as independent variables and total screen use as dependent variable. E.g., poorer mental health (B=0.29, 95% CI 0.06-0.52, p=.01), perceived stress (B=0.67, 95% CI 0.43-0.91, p<.001), social support (B=-0.32, 95% CI -0.59 to -0.04, p=.02), coping behaviors (B=-0.17, 95% CI -0.26 to -0.09, p<.001).

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients reported in Table 2: B values with 95% CIs and p-values for mental health, perceived stress, social support, coping behaviors.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis of ABCD COVID-19 data.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in JAMA Pediatrics, January 2022, Volume 176, Number 1.

FT_EC7_reason: Paper includes individual psychological/clinical predictors (mental health, perceived stress, coping behaviors, social support) as independent variables predicting screen use. FT-EC7 does not apply per criterion (a)(b) requiring ONLY demographic/contextual predictors.
notes: Meets all inclusion criteria. Cross-sectional analysis with specific screen modalities and inferential statistics. Individual-level psychological predictors rule out FT-EC7.

FT_IC1.evidence: Uses ABCD Study COVID-19 survey data collected May 16-22, 2020 (ABCD Data Release 3.0) with N=5335 adolescents ages 10-14.

FT_IC2.evidence: Measures specific recreational screen time categories: multiplayer gaming, texting, social media, video chatting. Adolescents and parents reported hours per day across 5 screen categories excluding schoolwork.

FT_IC3.evidence: Linear regression analyses testing associations between sociodemographic characteristics and absolute differences in parent- vs. adolescent-reported screen time (Table 4). E.g., age (B=0.20, 95% CI 0.11-0.29, p<.001), male sex (B=0.88, 95% CI 0.78-0.98, p<.001 for multiplayer gaming), Latino/Hispanic race (B=0.30, 95% CI 0.02-0.58, p=.04), Black race (B=0.68, 95% CI 0.36-1.00, p<.001). Also paired t-tests comparing parent- and adolescent-reported screen time.

FT_IC4.evidence: Multiple regression coefficients reported in Table 4 with 95% CIs and p-values for age, sex, race/ethnicity, country of birth, language, marital status, education, income across screen time categories.

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical cross-sectional analysis comparing parent and adolescent reports of recreational screen time.

FT_IC6.evidence: Published journal article in Academic Pediatrics, April 2022, Volume 22, Number 3.

FT_EC7_reason: Cross-sectional analysis examining sociodemographic predictors of parent-adolescent screen time discrepancies. Does not fit FT-EC7 pattern (not a longitudinal prospective design predicting future SM use from baseline demographics; analysis is descriptive of discrepancies not directionality per se).
notes: Meets all inclusion criteria. Cross-sectional analysis with specific screen modalities measured via paired parent-adolescent reports. Multiple regression analyses with sociodemographic predictors.
